text: We describe a case of combined robotic liver and rectal resection for an adenocarcinoma of the high rectum with a synchronous liver metastasis.
The patient is a seventy-two years old male presenting with rectal bleeding and anemia.
The clinical assessment reveals an adenocarcinoma of the high rectum with a three centimeter synchronous liver metastasis located between S seven and S eight and classified as CT three N zero M one.
After a multidisciplinary team discussion, a neoadjuvant chemotherapy with Fofox was started.
The restaging after three moths of chemotherapy showed a partial response according to resist criteria.
The UMARCS score showed an high surgical difficulty for the rectal resection.
A combined liver wedge resection and rectal resection with partial mesorectal excision was planned.

The procedure begins with the hepatic resection. The da Vinci robotic system is positioned on the patient's left side, while the assistant surgeon stands between the patient's legs, preparing for the operation.
The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum through a five-centimeter incision using an Alexis retractor, visibly expanding the abdominal cavity for surgical access.
Four 8mm robotic trocars and one 12mm laparoscopic trocar are positioned in the abdominal wall, providing access for robotic instruments and the laparoscopic camera.
The surgeon opens the hepatogastric ligament and places a tunique with a Foley catheter around the hepatic pedicle, preparing for further manipulation of the liver structures.
The surgeon uses an intraoperative ultrasound to locate the liver lesion and visually demarcate the resection margins on the hepatic tissue.
The intraoperative ultrasound displays a three-centimeter lesion in the right hepatic lobe, showing no connection with the principal portal pedicles or hepatic veins.
The surgeon cuts the coronary ligament and begins an atypical hepatectomy of segments seven and eight, guided by intraoperative ultrasound.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using a monopolar hook, bipolar forceps, and a vessel sealer, carefully dividing the liver tissue.
Hemlock clips are applied to the major portal and hepatic veins to achieve effective hemostasis during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs parenchymal transection using two intermittent intracorporeal Pringle maneuvers, each lasting fifteen minutes, to control blood flow during the hepatectomy.
The surgeon identifies, clips, and sections two main subsegmental portal pedicles and three hepatic veins to complete the hepatic resection.
After achieving hemostasis, the surgeon extracts the surgical specimen through the fan and steel incision. The procedure concludes with the rectal resection.

The surgeon repositions robotic ports one and two using a twelve millimeter and eight millimeter robotic trocar. The robotic boom is adjusted to focus on a new target anatomy within the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an extended medial to lateral approach during the hepatectomy, progressing through the liver tissue with visible precision.
The surgeon performs dissection while preserving the Giraud's fascia, revealing the left ureter.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery 1.5 centimeters from the aorta, clips it with a Hemolock, and then sections the artery.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric vein, applies clips to secure it, and then sections the vein using surgical instruments.
The surgeon continues the dissection, revealing the lower border of the pancreas, with clear visualization of the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an extended medial-to-lateral approach to mobilize the splenic flexure, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon separates the superior surface of the pancreas from the retroperitoneum, revealing the lesser sac as it is opened.
The surgeon mobilizes the left colon by dissecting the Toldt's fascia using monopolar scissors and a vessel sealer, carefully separating the colon from the underlying structures.
The surgeon begins the PME by dissecting the posterior plane, followed by the right and left planes, using precise surgical techniques to separate these anatomical structures.
The surgeon visualizes the tumor on the anterior surface of the rectum and performs mesorectal dissection five centimeters distal to the rectal lesion.
The surgeon completes the procedure by performing a stapled end-to-end colorectal anastomosis, joining the ends of the colon with a surgical stapler.
The surgeon creates a diverting ileostomy at the site of robotic port number two, preparing the intestinal segment for diversion.
Overall operative time was three hundred ten minutes.
The postoperative period was uneventful.
The patient was discharged at postoperative day seven.

The histological exam, showed an R-zero resection of an PT-III and one AM-I adenocarcinoma, with one out of fifteen positive lymph nodes, and, a R-zero resection of a three-centimeter colorectal liver metastasis.
